title: Will the Boat Sink the Water
description: Will the Boat Sink the Water is a academic study by Chen Guidi and Wu Chuntao. Banned for exploring peasant protests.
descriptionParagraphs: Will the Boat Sink the Water is a academic study by Chen Guidi and Wu Chuntao. Banned for exploring peasant protests.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a academic study, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in China. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned for exploring peasant protests. Sold an estimated 7 million pirated copies, despite being almost immediately banned by China's propaganda department. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Will the Boat Sink the Water is a academic study by Chen Guidi and Wu Chuntao. Banned for exploring peasant protests.
banningParagraphs: Will the Boat Sink the Water entered censorship debates as a academic study associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th-21st century in China, where Chinese state censors and party authorities banned or suppressed publication. Banned for exploring peasant protests. Sold an estimated 7 million pirated copies, despite being almost immediately banned by China's propaganda department. Banned for exploring peasant protests. Sold an estimated 7 million pirated copies, despite being almost immediately banned by China's propaganda department.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
